questions.question:
The central idea of “Everybody Jump” is that humans affect each other more than they affect the planet. How does Munroe's use of text structure help develop this central idea? Choose one specific way Munroe structures the text and explain how it develops the central idea.


Respond in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, two pieces of evidence, and an explanation.

responses.bucket_rationale: The response is a single period ('.'), which is less than 20 characters and does not attempt to answer the question. This fits the 'minimal effort' disqualifier for Score 0. There is no evidence, claim, or analysis present.

responses.content: cause-and-effect text structure to demonstrate that human interaction has a far greater impact than our collective physical force on the planet

In "Everybody Jump" (from What If?), Randall Munroe uses a cause-and-effect text structure to demonstrate that human interaction has a far greater impact than our collective physical force on the planet. He begins by mathematically debunking the idea that a synchronized jump could shift the Earth’s orbit, noting that the planet is so massive the jump would only result in a "recoil" less than the width of an atom. After establishing this lack of physical impact, Munroe shifts the focus to the human aftermath, describing how the "real effects" occur when billions of people find themselves stranded in one location with no infrastructure. He explains that "within weeks, billions of people will be dead" due to the logistical impossibility of feeding or transporting the crowd, effectively proving his point. By contrasting the negligible physical effect on the Earth with the catastrophic social and biological consequences of human proximity, Munroe’s structure emphasizes that our greatest influence lies in our interconnectedness rather than our physical power.

He begins by mathematically debunking the idea that a synchronized jump could shift the Earth’s orbit, noting that the planet is so massive the jump would only result in a "recoil" less than the width of an atom. After establishing this lack of physical impact, Munroe shifts the focus to the human aftermath, describing how the "real effects" occur when billions of people find themselves stranded in one location with no infrastructure. He explains that "within weeks, billions of people will be dead" due to the logistical impossibility of feeding or transporting the crowd, effectively proving his point. By contrasting the negligible physical effect on the Earth with the catastrophic social and biological consequences of human proximity, Munroe’s structure emphasizes that our greatest influence lies in our interconnectedness rather than our physical power.

questions.question: Based on paragraphs 10-12, what can you infer was important to Margaret as a photojournalist?

responses.content: Her mother gave Margaret her first camera in 1921, when she was 17 years old. Her interest in photography grew as a result of her father’s enthusiasm for cameras. A few years later, Margaret’s classmates at Cornell University became her first admirers when photos she took of the campus appeared in the school newspapers.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is too long and does not specifically show an example of Margaret Bourke-White's hardworking attitude helping her career. The correct answer should come from the section about her work at the steel mill, where her determination and problem-solving are shown.

responses.content: At first, several people at the company wondered if a woman could stand up to the intense heat and generally dirty and gritty conditions inside a steel mill. When Margaret finally got permission, the technical problems began. Black-and-white film at that time was sensitive to blue light, not the reds and oranges of hot steel. The pictures came out all black. Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes

The pictures came out all black. Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes.'
